petitioner_background: The petitioner is a female martial artist (Tai Chi practitioner) from China seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in athletics under INA § 203(b)(1)(A). She entered the United States in December 2002 on a B-2 nonimmigrant visa and intends to open an elementary martial arts training center in New York City rather than continue competing as an athlete. She has competed in Tai Chi competitions in China and internationally, authored a Tai Chi textbook, and held memberships in various Qingdao-based martial arts committees.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Two first place prizes at the Fifth International Chinese Martial Arts Tournament of Canton in 2001 (in form and another category), sponsored by a Hall of Fame for Martial Arts in Ohio, which certified 400 competitors 'from almost every country' with only three awards issued per category; first prize, Female 'Group B' at the Qingdao International Wushu Championship in July 2002 (certified as having 580 competitors from over 50 'countries or districts' with three awards in the female Tai Chi Sword group); first place in the Female elder group at the Qingdao 'Wan Tong Cup' national Tai Chi Fest and Tai Chi Sword competition in November 2000 (300 competitors, 40 in advanced levels, three levels of awards); brochure for the 2002 China Qingdao Fourth International Wushu Championship; two affidavits from Jin Wang regarding Internet search engine hits (4,110 entries for Qingdao International Wushu Competition and 51 entries for 'Hall of Fame'/International Chinese Martial Arts Tournament of Canton); newspaper articles dated July 11 and 12, 2002; a list of students submitted on appeal.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found significant discrepancies undermining the evidence. The 2002 Qingdao brochure listed Group B as juveniles aged 13-18, but the petitioner (well over 18 in 2002) was listed in the program as Group F (elder, 51-60), yet her certificate and a national referee's reference letter claimed she won in 'Group B' — an unresolved inconsistency under Matter of Ho, 19 I&N Dec. 582, rendering the certificate suspect and reducing the referee letter's evidentiary value. The newspaper articles confirmed her participation but did not mention any awards won, with no explanation for the omission. The AAO also noted the record contained no evidence the petitioner was even in the United States in October 2001 when the International Chinese Martial Arts Tournament of Canton took place in Ohio (her passport was issued June 17, 2002). The record contained little documentation on the significance of the 2000 Wan Tong Cup. Additionally, because the petitioner intends to work as an instructor, the AAO noted that student awards could constitute comparable evidence under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(4), but the record contained no evidence that any student won nationally or internationally recognized awards under her tutelage.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence of membership in the Qingdao Tai Chi Fist Committee as of 1997 and the Qingdao Hai Jian Tai Chi Fist Club as of 2001; a 'partial English translation' of the club rules for Qingdao Hai Jian Tai Chi Fist Club (membership chapter not translated); a statement from the president of the Qingdao Martial Arts Committee asserting the Qingdao committee is 'one of the best' and limits membership to those 'who have received outstanding award and [are] very proficient'; a certificate from the Qingdao Tai Chi Fist Committee limiting membership to those 'who have been granted awards and possess very high talents'; on appeal, evidence of membership in the Chinese Tai Chi Chuan Expert Association as of August 2002, whose certificate limited membership to those with 'Tai Chi Chuan Expert Qualification' who 'have achieved extraordinary achievements in the recent national and international Tai Chi Chuan competitions.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the Qingdao memberships to be local by definition, and the petitioner did not establish they required outstanding achievements as judged by recognized national or international experts. The national Chinese Tai Chi Chuan Expert Association membership was submitted for the first time on appeal, which the AAO refused to accept under Matter of Soriano, 19 I&N Dec. 764, and Matter of Obaigbena, 19 I&N Dec. 533, which disfavor new evidence on appeal after a petitioner has been put on notice of deficiencies. The AAO noted this was part of a pattern where the petitioner responded to concerns about one accomplishment by submitting evidence of a different one. Regardless, without official bylaws listing specific criteria (rather than general phrases like 'extraordinary achievements'), the AAO could not determine whether the association truly required outstanding achievements of all members. The membership also appeared to relate to her abilities as an instructor.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Two articles submitted on appeal, primarily about the Qingdao International Wushu Championship, published in Da Zhong Daily (July 11, 2002) and Qingdao Daily (July 12, 2002).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the articles did not mention that the petitioner won any awards, only that she participated. The articles were primarily about the competition itself, not about the petitioner. The petitioner did not provide evidence that either newspaper had a national circulation, so she failed to establish the articles appeared in major media. Additionally, the articles were not about the petitioner's abilities as an instructor (her intended field of work in the U.S.).

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: A certificate appointing the petitioner as a 'First Grade Instructor of Shandong Province Martial Art School' allowing her to teach Tai Chi Chuan within Shandong Province and participate in grading, training, and related competition/exchange activities; a certificate from the Qingdao Martial Arts Committee confirming the petitioner served 'as the reviewer for the national competition of Tai Chi Fist and Tai Chi Sword since 1999.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director did not directly address this criterion, and the petitioner submitted no new evidence on appeal other than her expert membership. Without evidence that the petitioner has judged competitions outside of Qingdao, the AAO could not conclude that her judging positions were indicative of or consistent with national acclaim.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Several reference letters from representatives of the Qingdao Martial Arts Committee and the Qingdao Tai Chi Committee. A Vice Chairman attested to the petitioner's ability and noted she had authored a book and refereed competitions, while conceding Tai Chi originated thousands of years ago. A national referee asserted he had learned from the petitioner, that she comes up with 'unique ideas,' and that he had applied her opinions to his practice. A Vice President of the Qingdao Tai Chi Committee asserted the petitioner had 'developed new actions or styles, which enrich the practice of the martial arts.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that none of the letter writers identified a specific contribution made by the petitioner. The Vice Chairman did not identify any specific contribution to Tai Chi. The national referee did not identify a specific 'unique idea' or explain how her ideas had influenced Tai Chi to constitute original contributions of major significance. The Vice President did not explain the new actions or styles or provide examples of how they had influenced Tai Chi practice. The director noted that letters from independent experts who did not know the petitioner personally would have been more persuasive, but the petitioner submitted no more independent letters on appeal. The letters failed to identify a specific contribution that had a major impact on the practice of Tai Chi either as an athlete or instructor.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Copies of pages from a textbook authored by the petitioner entitled 'One hundred eight Tai-Chi Fuhu Zhang'; on appeal, a second book for which she was coauthor, '42 Series of Tai Chi Sword and Its Application in Daily Life.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director concluded the petitioner had not established the impact of her book in the field. On appeal, rather than addressing the significance of the first book, the petitioner submitted a second book — continuing the pattern of responding to concerns about one accomplishment with evidence of a different accomplishment. The petitioner failed to demonstrate the significance of either book; specifically, the record contained no evidence that either book was a bestseller or otherwise influential in the Tai Chi community nationally.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: A certificate from the Qingdao Martial Arts Committee affirming that it 'has hired [the petitioner] as the endorser for this field since 1999.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director inquired whether the petitioner had been a paid endorser for the sport itself and concluded the record lacked evidence she was a paid spokesman for an athletic company. The petitioner did not address this criterion on appeal. The AAO found a general claim of being an 'endorser' could not meet this criterion, which under the plain language of 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(x) requires specific sales numbers. The record also lacked sales data for the petitioner's book.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal, concluding the petitioner failed to establish sustained national or international acclaim or that she was within the small percentage at the very top of her field. The evidence showed talent as a martial artist but was not persuasive that her achievements set her significantly above almost all other martial art instructors. Additionally, the AAO found that competitive athletics and coaching are not the same area of expertise under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h), and the petitioner intended to work as a coach/trainer rather than as an athlete, though the AAO did not need to resolve this issue because she had not established acclaim as an athlete in the first place. Finally, the petitioner's general statement of intent to open a training center in New York City lacked any explanation of how she would pursue this intent or identify financing sources, failing the requirement under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(5) to show she is coming to continue work in her area of expertise. The burden of proof under INA § 291 remained with the petitioner, and she did not sustain it.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Lee v. I.N.S., 237 F. Supp. 2d 914 (N.D. Ill. 2002)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The AAO identified credibility concerns under Matter of Ho regarding an unresolved discrepancy between the Qingdao competition brochure (listing the petitioner in Group F, elder age 51-60) and the award certificate and a referee's letter (both claiming she won in Group B, juvenile age 13-18), rendering the award certificate suspect and reducing the referee letter's evidentiary value. The AAO noted that newspaper articles dated during the competition confirmed her participation but did not mention any awards, with no explanation for the omission. The record also contained no evidence the petitioner was in the United States in October 2001 when the International Chinese Martial Arts Tournament of Canton took place in Ohio (her passport was issued June 17, 2002). The AAO identified a pattern where the petitioner responded to director concerns about significance of one accomplishment by submitting evidence of different accomplishments. Procedurally, the AAO declined to consider new evidence (the Chinese Tai Chi Chuan Expert Association membership) submitted for the first time on appeal under Matter of Soriano and Matter of Obaigbena.
